era_context: The mid-15th century is a period of significant transition in Europe and Asia. The Hundred Years' War between England and France has recently ended (1453), reshaping Western European politics. The Ottoman Empire is consolidating power after the conquest of Constantinople. The Ming Dynasty in China is stable under centralised rule. The Italian Renaissance is flourishing. In Africa, Portuguese explorers are establishing trading posts along the coast.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Indigenous Americas (no written records in European tradition)

events.title: Venetian-Ottoman War (First Venetian-Ottoman War) continues
events.description: The First Venetian-Ottoman War (1463–1479) was ongoing in 1463. Venice, a major Mediterranean naval power, was in conflict with the expanding Ottoman Empire over control of trade routes and territories in the Eastern Mediterranean. The war was costly for both sides and would ultimately result in Ottoman victory and Venetian territorial losses. In 1463, the conflict was in its early stages, with naval battles and sieges occurring in the Aegean and Adriatic.
key_figures: Venetian Republic (represented by various doges and commanders)
events.certainty_note: The First Venetian-Ottoman War is well-documented in both Venetian and Ottoman sources.

events.title: Thirteen Years' War between Poland-Lithuania and Teutonic Order continues
events.description: The Thirteen Years' War (1454–1466) between the Polish-Lithuanian Commonwealth and the Teutonic Order was ongoing in 1463. The conflict had been initiated by the Prussian Confederation's appeal to Poland against Teutonic rule. In 1463, both sides were engaged in military campaigns and diplomatic negotiations. The war would continue for three more years before concluding with the Peace of Thorn in 1466.
key_figures: Ludwig von Vietinghoff (Grand Master of the Teutonic Order)
events.certainty_note: The Thirteen Years' War is well-documented; 1463 falls within the confirmed period of conflict.

events.title: Zhengtong Emperor's reign in Ming China; political consolidation
events.description: The Zhengtong Emperor (Zhu Qizhen) was ruling the Ming Dynasty in 1463. He had been restored to the throne in 1457 after a period of captivity and deposition. His reign was marked by administrative consolidation and the continuation of Ming centralised rule. In 1463, the Ming Dynasty was stable and prosperous, with the emperor consolidating his authority. However, his reign would be marked by the growing influence of eunuchs in court politics.

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming Dynasty)

events.certainty_note: The Zhengtong Emperor's reign is well-documented in Ming court records.
cross_references: Part of Ming Dynasty political history (1368–1644)

events.title: Mehmed II consolidates Ottoman power; campaigns continue
events.description: Mehmed II, the Conqueror, continued his consolidation of Ottoman power in 1463. Following the conquest of Constantinople in 1453, Mehmed was systematically subjugating rival powers in Anatolia and the Balkans. In 1463, he was engaged in military campaigns to expand Ottoman territory and eliminate threats to Ottoman hegemony. His campaigns against Venice and other powers were part of a broader strategy to establish Ottoman dominance in the Eastern Mediterranean.
events.certainty_note: Mehmed II's military activities are well-documented in Ottoman and Venetian sources.

events.title: Italian Renaissance: Piero della Francesca and Sandro Botticelli active
events.description: In 1463, major figures of the Italian Renaissance were active and producing significant works. Piero della Francesca was completing major commissions in Urbino and Arezzo, while Sandro Botticelli was establishing himself as a major painter in Florence. The year 1463 was a period of intense artistic activity and innovation in Italy, with the Renaissance in full flourish. These artists were developing new techniques in perspective, light, and form that would define Renaissance art.
key_figures: Federico da Montefeltro (Duke of Urbino, patron)

sources.name: Giorgio Vasari, Lives of the Artists

events.certainty_note: The careers of Piero della Francesca and Sandro Botticelli are well-documented, though specific works and dates can sometimes vary.
cross_references: Part of Italian Renaissance cultural flourishing (14th–16th centuries)

events.title: Portuguese exploration: continued expansion along African coast
events.description: Portuguese explorers, under the patronage of Prince Henry the Navigator, continued their systematic exploration of the African coast in 1463. Portuguese trading posts were being established along the Guinea coast, and explorers were pushing further south. These expeditions were laying the groundwork for the later Age of Exploration and establishing Portuguese commercial dominance in West African trade.

sources.name: Portuguese royal archives and navigation records

events.certainty_note: Portuguese exploration of this period is documented, though specific dates for individual voyages can vary.
cross_references: Part of Portuguese Age of Exploration (1415–1500s)

disconfirming_evidence: The characterisation of the First Venetian-Ottoman War as a straightforward conflict between two powers is somewhat simplified. Modern scholarship emphasises the complex political and economic factors driving the conflict, including Venetian commercial interests, Ottoman expansion, and the involvement of other Mediterranean powers. Additionally, the role of Venice as a 'major Mediterranean naval power' in 1463 is somewhat overstated; by this time, Venice's naval dominance was already being challenged by Ottoman expansion.
historiographic_note: The year 1463 is well-documented in European sources, particularly for military and political events. Italian Renaissance sources are particularly rich. Ming Dynasty records are extensive and contemporary. However, as with other years in this period, non-European regions are underrepresented. The sources available are often written from the perspective of European powers or East Asian courts. The historical record for 1463 is heavily weighted toward European and East Asian events, with limited documentation of events in Africa, the Americas, and Central Asia.

graph_edges.note: The First Venetian-Ottoman War continued until 1479
The Thirteen Years' War continued and concluded in 1466
Zhengtong Emperor's reign ended in 1464 with succession of Chenghua Emperor
